data.title: MSCI: Financial Services 'Winter' Won't Last For Long
data.description:
We all know that market preferences can change on a dime. One year is all about software, another is all about semiconductors, and sometimes it's banks. These days, of course, it's all about AI.

Smart investors exploit opportunities in overlooked sectors before they return to fashion. One industry that's being left behind these days is financial information services, which have broadly underperformed.

And while there are valid arguments against the sector as a whole, I argue MSCI (MSCI) is largely unaffected. With the stock trading nearly 20% below its historical valuation, despite a strong growth outlook, I see MSCI as an attractive play on an overlooked sector..

Let's dive in.

Financial Information Services 'Winter'

The financial information services sector is home to some of the most well-known long-term compounders in the market, including S&P Global (SPGI), Moody's (MCO), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), Fair Isaac (FICO), and MSCI.

The throughline that I see between those companies is that they provide unique and differentiated services that are enablers of financial transactions, but they do so without taking the risk upon themselves. This places these companies in a unique position to consistently capitalize on global GDP growth while carrying a lower risk.

For instance, Moody's gives a credit rating, which plays a huge role in the credit market, but Moody's does not provide credit. Fair Isaac is doing the same thing in consumer lending. S&P Global is creating indices and benchmarks that are used for ETFs, but it isn't providing the ETF.

Another throughline of these companies, apparently, is that they're generally out of favor from the market's perspective in recent years. In fact, 100% of them have underperformed the market over the past year.

Even further, everyone except FICO has underperformed the market on a five-year and a three-year basis as well. Clearly, the market doesn't like these names too much.

Looking For Explanation

The way I see it, three main reasons have caused this performance. One, starting valuations. Two, AI and disruption fears.

Valuations

Even after five years of underperformance, all the names on the list trade above 30 times earnings, and as we can see on the graph, this reflects meaningful multiple contraction over the period, which means they started even higher.

Compared to the S&P 500's 26x multiple, the bar for our financial info basket was and still is higher.

AI & Disruption

When we're talking about companies with decades of compounding, it's easy to gloss over every risk that comes along and tag it as insignificant. Frankly, with the list we're talking about, 9 times out of 10, you'll be correct to shrug away disruption risk claims.

Still, it's undeniable that the rise of AI, stablecoins, and crypto is at the very least a good argument against continued dominance.

In MSCI's case, we're talking about a company that generates about 44% of its sales from information services that are billed primarily on a per-seat basis. If the number of seats across the industry shrinks due to AI or simply grows more slowly, this is a cause for concern.

The rest of the business is coming from fees MSCI collects primarily from ETF providers that are using its indices. Although this is quite improbable in my view, some people believe that the way people buy equities will change in the future as we'll move to 24/7 tokenized markets.

The Stock Underperforms, Fears Are High, But Results Remain Strong

With the above backdrop in mind, it's important to look at the numbers and see if the negative sentiment is actually materializing in fundamentals.

In the recently announced third quarter, MSCI beat EPS estimates and came very slightly below revenue expectations.

Total revenues grew 9.5% Y/Y, a slight acceleration from Q2. Index revenue rose 11% to $450 million, Analytics increased 6% to $180 million, Sustainability rose 8% (5% organically), and Private markets grew 10% (8% organically).

Meanwhile, it's important to remember that the majority of MSCI's revenues are coming from recurring arrangements with renewal rates in the vicinity of 95%. If things were so terrible in the industry, you would expect retention rates to go down, but we can clearly see they are close to record levels.

Further, MSCI is not only maintaining strong high-single-digit growth, but it is doing so while expanding margins and accelerating buybacks, with operating margins improving by 100 bps Y/Y, and share repurchases totaling $1.2 billion in the quarter, the highest in the company's history.

This has led to 16% growth in EPS, and also means that MSCI now has a 4.5% buyback yield over the past twelve months.

All in all, nothing here says the company is struggling. To me, the opposite is true.

Valuation & Outlook

When I first started covering MSCI, I held a bearish view, primarily because of its relatively high valuation at the time. Today, the story is quite different.

At $585 per share, MSCI trades at 30 times 2026 estimates, far below the company's five-year average of 42.

I previously argued that it is unreasonable to assume MSCI will return to its historical multiple, simply because I didn't expect the company to return to its historical EPS growth rates, which were higher than 20%.

Today, consensus sees MSCI growing EPS by about 12.5% annually going forward, which I find a bit too low. Revenue is set to grow by about 9.5%, margins will gradually improve, and buybacks are accelerating.

That said, if we assume that the 12.5% growth is correct, then we're getting the stock at around 2.4x PEG. Not a bargain, but not as high as the 3x we saw previously, and around the same levels as S&P Global and Moody's.

In my mind, this is already an attractive valuation to buy at, especially as I see significant room for upward revisions. My price target of $650 is based on a 30x multiple on 2027 estimates. That said, $550 per share is where I'd go really aggressive.

Conclusion

Financial information services stocks are struggling, with underperformance across the board. MSCI is no exception, with the stock severely lagging on a five-year, three-year, and 1-year basis.

I believe the issues with the stock are elevated starting valuations and AI fears. The first issue is slowly solving itself, as the stock hasn't moved while EPS has grown materially. The second issue is in my mind more about sentiment than actual concerns.

I see MSCI growing EPS faster than 13% annually in the mid-term, and expect the stock to reach a price target of $650 by the end of 2026.

As such, I reiterate a 'Buy' rating.

data.title: EWC: A Primer On The Oldest Canadian ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The iShares MSCI Canada ETF

The iShares MSCI Canada ETF (EWC), which is one of the oldest country-specific ETFs around (it was set-up in March 1996), is a product of the iShares stable of ETFs that belongs to Blackrock, Inc. EWC, which is on the cusp of hitting three decades as a listed product, has managed to amass assets under management of nearly $3.5B during its lifespan. This Canadian equities focused product has a dividend yields of 1.68% (distributions are made twice a year), and is priced at an expense ratio of 0.5%, which is in line with the median ETF threshold,

What Does EWC Do?

EWC, through a process known as representative sampling, tracks an index called MSCI Canada Custom Capped Index [MCCCI] in its efforts to cover Canadian stocks.

For those not familiar with the term, representative sampling is generally employed when a certain tracking index consists of many hundreds and thousands of stocks. It wouldn’t be hugely cost efficient (consider the degree of elevated trading costs) to try and replicate that index by owning the exact same stocks and in the same proportion. So rather, one chooses a smaller pool of stocks, which in aggregate, jointly mirror the qualities of that index (be it in terms of market-cap breakup, the risk profile, overall industry exposure, etc.). In MCCCI’s case, which only consists of 83 stocks, we don’t see any major reason why EWC can’t resort to full replication.

Nonetheless, it doesn’t appear that EWC’s returns vary a great deal from the returns of MCCCI. This is validated by the tracking error readings that are at an acceptable threshold and in line with what other ETFs experience (although over the past year, it has been wider that normal). A wide tracking error tells you that a passive ETFs returns tend to deviate more from the tracking index’s returns.

So, coming back to MCCCI, what does it consist of? Well, it is essentially a variation of the famed MSCI Canada Index [MCI], which represents the stocks that comprise the top 85% of Canada’s free-float market-cap. MCCI differs from MCI, in that, it incorporates certain caps to prevent the concentration of group entities (stocks that belong to the same corporate group). To elaborate, every quarter, MCCCI ensures that no single group entity can exceed 22.5% of the index, and also all group entities with individual weights of over 5%, are not allowed to jointly account for over 24.5% of the index.

What Are The Key Traits of EWC’s Portfolio?

EWC’s tracking index-MCCCI, may impose caps, which may initially give investors the impression of it being a well-spread-out portfolio, but do consider that MCI, which it is based on, is supposed to represent the top 85% of Canadian stocks. As a result, investors are not going to get any small or micro-cap exposure by pursuing EWC. And since MCI is a market-cap weighted index, naturally the largest stocks will have greater clout.

Note that 53% of EWC’s stocks consists of giant-caps alone, while its average market-cap of $57B also makes it come across as a giant-cap tilted proxy.

From a stylistic perspective, EWC appears to offer something for different types of investors, with a healthy component of growth and value stocks. Crucially, it is stocks with blended characteristics or GARP-style stocks (stocks with above average growth potential that are typically also trading at discounted valuations) that dominate with an aggregate weight of 37%.

As far as sector exposure goes, one gets to dabble with 10 different sectors, but two things stand out. Firstly, EWC tilts very heavily to just one sector- financials, which take up 37% of the portfolio, which is more than twice the exposure of the next biggest sector- energy. Secondly, EWC barely offers any exposure to real estate stocks, with even its idle cash equating to a higher stake!

What Are The Risks Associated With EWC?

EWC is dollar-denominated, but its holdings are denominated in Canadian dollars [CAD], so the potential weakening of the CAD against the USD could end up damaging the returns that EWC holders eventually get, even if EWC’s holdings in CAD terms or local currency terms do well, or stay flat. Now, assuming all else is equal, The Bank of Canada (Central Bank of Canada) cuts policy rates, and the US Fed (US central bank) lifts policy rates, this will make CAD denominated assets less attractive, while boosting the allure of USD denominated assets. This will eventually result in the weakening of the CAD against the USD. Also consider that a hefty chunk of this portfolio consists of financial stocks, their net interest income [NII] profile will also be under pressure in an era of relatively low interest rates, potentially resulting in lower profits, if they can’t make up for the short-fall from non-interest income avenues, and higher cost efficiencies.

If your portfolio is inundated with US-based stocks and you’re looking for some geographical diversification away from this terrain towards other developed markets, we don’t believe EWC would represent the most optimal alternative. We say this because EWC’s beta (a measure of sensitivity of one product, versus the movements of another) relative to the S&P 500, suggests that its moves in close tandem with the latter.

As you can see from the image above, EWC’s beta of 0.96x is quite elevated (amongst the major developed market ETFs, only the Australian product has a higher beta), and suggests that for every $1 movement in the S&P 500, EWC will typically move by $0.96!

The US is also Canada’s largest trading partner and has traditionally accounted for over three-fourths of the latter’s total exports. This makes EWC and its holdings extremely vulnerable to stringent foreign policy and tariff measures imposed by the US, particularly under the leadership of President Trump. Already, as things stand, Canada is the only G7 country that has not been able to strike a deal with the US since Trump started imposing tariffs on its major trading partners. Ongoing rumblings here, will force Canada to start turning to other major super powers like China, and compromise on some of the initiatives it had previously taken to protect its domestic industries (for instance, there are suggestions that the 100% tariffs that were levied on Chinese EVs may now be scrapped)

Who Is EWC ETF For?

EWC’s top-3 sectors- financials, energy, and materials, all of which jointly account for two-thirds of the entire portfolio, are cyclical in nature. This makes EWC suitable for someone who is unfazed by the shifts in the overall economic cycle over time.

Since EWC is strongly exposed to the financial sector (37% of holdings), it also makes it suitable for those who are familiar and comfortable with gauging the monetary policy conditions of Canada. Financial stocks generally also have a reputation of distributing their excess capital by way of consistent and sturdy dividend payouts (there are quite a few dividend aristocrats), or by buying up their own stock, so investors who appreciate these qualities, rather than those who like to see capital reinvested back in a business will fancy EWC.

Canada is also a very resource-heavy economy, with products such as crude oil, natural gas, gold, lumber, etc. being exported across the globe. Thus, in many ways it can also be seen as an unorthodox way to play a boom or recovery in commodity prices.

EWC’s holdings are also substantially cheaper than its US counterparts (an approximate valuation discount of 28% to the S&P 500 on a price-to-earnings and price-to-cash flow basis), and since we know that EWC moves in close tandem with SPY (beta effect of nearly 1x), one could construe the former as a cheaper way to play the movement of US stocks.

Which Are The Peers of EWC?

The Canadian ETF terrain is not inundated with a plethora of options, and besides EWC, investors have the option of considering two other passively managed ETFs. The first is the JP Morgan BetaBuilders Canada ETF (BBCA), and the second is the Franklin FTSE Canada ETF (FLCA).

What we find interesting to note is that the youngest of these three products-BBCA which tracks an index called the Morningstar Canada Target Market Exposure [MCTME], currently appears to be the most popular for long-term investors, with an AUM [assets under management] approaching $9B, which is 2.6x that of our focus product (despite BBCA getting listed 21 years after EWC).

There’s no doubt that BBCA’s popularity vs EWC is driven by its much cheaper expense ratio of just 0.19%, but if cost-efficiency was the main driver then FLCA ought to have attracted the greatest flows as its expense ratio is not even half as much as what BBCA costs.

BBCA covers a slightly smaller pool of stocks than EWC (78 as opposed to 84 for EWC), with its average market-cap suggesting a tilt towards slightly larger names. It is also the only ETF from this bunch that distributes quarterly (EWC has the weakest income profile, with only semi-annual distributions and a yield that is much lower than FLCA).

FLCA which focuses on the FTSE Canada RIC Capped Index, we feel is something of an underappreciated product (it only has around half a billion of AUM). Not only does it offer the cheapest route to Canadian stocks, it also offers the highest yield, and so far, since BBCA got listed (the most recent listing of the three), it has outperformed the other two.

We don’t believe that FLCA’s superior returns have come by taking on more risk, as all three products have near-identical volatility profiles. They are all also very stable (only 3-4% of the portfolio gets turned over in a year), and all offer similar exposure to financial stocks, which dominate their portfolios.

One area where EWC has an edge over these other two products is its allure as a short-term tactical play, even for those who want to profit from its downside (currently 6% of EWC’s total shares outstanding consists of shares that have been sold short, reflecting its ability to also attract bears). Note that the difference between its bid and ask is very tight at just 0.02% (the same can’t be said about FLCA, whose bid-ask spread is 10x more) which enables investors to quickly get in and get out of positions without worrying about slippage risk (the risk of not getting budgeted prices during buying and selling).

Summary

EWC, a stalwart in offering Canadian equity exposure, is a cyclically themed, financial-heavy product that is priced at a significant valuation discount to US stocks. EWC is also very liquid, and its best-in-class bid-ask spreads also make it suitable for short-term tactical traders. Having said that, EWC may not be suitable for those who want useful diversification from US stocks, and its cost profile and its return track record aren’t as good as some of the newer Canadian alternatives.

This article answers these three main questions about EWC:

  • What are the main features of EWC’s portfolio?
  • What type of investor will be attracted to EWC, and what are the risks they could face?
  • What other Candian-focused ETFs are there for US-based investors besides EWC?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: KBWD: Concentrated In REITs And BDCs For A Competitive Yield
data.description:
Invesco KBW High Dividend Yield Financial ETF (KBWD) is a fund focused on the high dividend segment of the financial sector. Its selection criteria and weighting methodology make the fund primarily focused on asset classes within the segment that are rarely covered by traditional passive “finance” ETFs, such as REITs and BDCs. For this reason, it comes with above-average costs, which, however, reflect a very competitive distribution compared to other ETFs in the same sector. This makes it a fund more suitable for cash flow generation rather than value appreciation, and that is probably its main distinguishing feature. But let’s take a closer look at its characteristics.

KBWD: Intro & Definition

Invesco KBW High Dividend Yield Financial ETF (KBWD), is an ETF that belongs to the “Financial Income / High Dividend ETF” category with an AUM of $418.34 million.

It was started in 2010 with the goal of replicating the performance of U.S. financial companies with high dividend yield. To do this, it follows with quarterly rebalancing, and full replication method the benchmark KBW Nasdaq Financial Sector Dividend Yield Index.

It has a total expense ratio of 4.93%.

It should be understood that the management fees portion of this fee are only 0.35%, while the rest is Acquired Fund Fees (AFF). Its “financial/income oriented” nature increases costs that accompany the BDC and REIT holdings, which pass down their expenses to the fund. These holdings are also what amplifies yield; it is currently above 13%, distributed monthly.

To this is added an average bid/ask spread below 0.1%, and a bid/ask premium/discount of -0.06%, which indicates an apparent NAV stability.

What Does KBWD Do?

According to the prospectus, the fund’s focus is to select companies belonging to the “financial sector” with a distinctive dividend yield according to specific selection criteria:

  • minimum price of $5;
  • average daily volume ≥ 100,000 shares;
  • at least 20% public float;
  • regular dividend payment.

In this context, the term “financial sector” should be interpreted broadly because the selection sorted by dividend yield leads to the inclusion within the ETF not of the classic banking or insurance representations, but of a mix of “yield play,” mainly oriented around the world of BDCs and REITs. Not surprisingly, it behaves very similarly to these two categories.

Consequently, it does not show a trend close to Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB); rather, it aligns with other solutions such as VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and the REITs segment, which here, for simplicity, is represented by VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT). Considering that REITs and BDCs must distribute at least 90% of pre-tax income, this means that in a portfolio, it would fit better as an income-oriented portfolio solution than as a classic financial solution linked to capital appreciation. Keep in mind the coupons are mostly ordinary dividends, not qualified, which could result in worse taxation compared to traditional blue-chip dividends.

Who Is KBWD For?

If we associate this ETF with an income fund, the fund becomes suitable for cash flow strategies.

But be careful, the cash flow–oriented nature should not be confused with lower volatility: the standard deviation is above 14, and the annualized volatility is higher than 18%. Therefore, it is still quite a volatile fund compared to the average. Probably less suitable for those seeking price stability.

For this reason, KBWD would be used by many as a tactical vehicle (trading during positive phases), not only as a core holding, or alternatively, as a satellite vehicle for slightly more aggressive income-oriented portfolios.

If that’s the case, when does it become an interesting portfolio vehicle? The heaviest holdings in the fund (IVR, ARR, TWO) invest in fixed-rate MBS (or other securitized instruments). Therefore, KBWD will still be sensitive to interest rate trends.

In this case, it should be remembered that theoretically, these benefit from low-rate cycles, while in “higher for longer” contexts, profitability compresses.

This means that the ETF would potentially be more suitable for monetary easing contexts or narrowing yield spreads, not during restrictive phases.

Roughly speaking, one can reason in terms of inverse correlation with the Fed Funds and say that when the Fed Funds contract, the probability of having a stable price and still distinctive dividends is higher during rate-cutting phases (even if it is not always true, as we will see in the risks section).

KBWD Holdings

KBWD has approximately 40 holdings, 100% U.S.-based, which are rebalanced quarterly following specific weighting criteria:

  • maximum 8% per stock (top 5 components);
  • the other holdings are “capped” at 4%.

This leads the ETF to have a Weighted Market Cap of about $3.4 billion, but with good diversification considering that only 36% of the holdings are in its top 10.

What makes the distribution more unbalanced is the sector allocation, which today can be divided as follows:

  • Mortgage REITs → 44.4%
  • Asset Management & Custody Banks → 40.5%
  • Regional Banks → 9.0%
  • Transaction & Payment Services → 2.5%
  • Life & Health Insurance → 2.1%
  • Consumer Finance → 1.5%

It is immediately noticeable that almost half of the portfolio is concentrated in a single sector (Mortgage REITs) and another 40% in BDCs.

And it is important to remember this because it gives the fund certain distinctive characteristics:

  • 94% are small caps and 6% are mid caps.

  • The P/E and fwd P/E are outside the financial benchmark, currently 11.2x and 7.57x respectively.

  • The 30-day SEC yield is 13.05%, certainly a distinctive yield.

The reason for this is mainly the fact that the underlying companies (Mortgage REITs and BDCs) use leverage of 5–10x, so the fund indirectly replicates a highly levered portfolio.

For this reason, investors in the fund should all the more keep an eye on the nature of the redistribution: if the fund were to distribute over time ROC instead of true income, the risk would be NAV erosion.

Peer Comparison

In order to make the previously reported list even more exhaustive, it makes sense to include in the peer table other reference points from the REITs market, in this case REM and the classic Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), and in the financial market the classic The Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF).

On the dividend side, it can be seen how KBWD clearly differs from the traditional financial sector, moving toward the territory of BDCs and a specific category of REITs, those better identified by REM (mortgages). In this sense, KBWD’s distribution is competitive compared to the rest of the peers.

Moving to the performance side, it can be seen how this leads to a deterioration in terms of price. The performance here is also closer to the REITs and BDC sectors.

This leads to lower total returns compared to the classic financial segment, but close to the two discussed sectors. As of today, KBWD maintains a competitive performance compared to them, but at a historical level, there is still a clear proximity and proportionality.

To conclude, KBWD also seems to win on the concentration & risk side, where, with more holdings compared to the financial, REITs, or BDC segments taken individually, it has less top 10 concentration.

Risks

Following this direction, we can say that KBWD has a hidden concentration risk that cannot go unnoticed: over 87% of the portfolio is concentrated in two sectors: Mortgage REITs and Asset Management/BDC. This amplifies its strong connection with the money market: simply because of the internal (not external) leverage effect, which is reflected in the connection of the underlying assets to interest rates (and partly to credit risk). Because REITs remain exposed to the Mortgage sector (hence the connection with rates), while BDCs are companies that invest in private operating companies in the credit market (often).

Therefore, an increase in interest rates would probably not play in favor of KBWD.

If, in addition to this, we consider high payouts to justify yields >12%, increasing attention must be paid to the nature of the distributions; because high payouts increase the probability of future distribution cuts, especially in contexts of declining earnings for mortgage REITs. An element that an income-seeking investor should take into account.

Pros & Cons

To conclude, I think KBWD has some quite evident advantages and distinctive traits:

  1. High yield compared to the average, today above 13%, distributed monthly: two elements highly appreciated by income-oriented investors.
  2. Targeted exposure to BDCs and REITs, which makes KBWD today more diversified and profitable than the single solutions trying to replicate those two markets.
  3. There is potential benefit in case of continuation of the Fed’s monetary easing process, considering the nature of KBWD’s holdings.

Of course, it would be inaccurate not to list the disadvantages as well:

  1. The counterbalance of the competitive yield is a constantly contracting price, also due to a distinctive TER, which drags down total returns compared to classic financial components.
  2. Its diversification finds a limit in the fact that 85% of the portfolio is in just those two sectors (Mortgage REITs and BDC); which makes KBWD a particularly cyclical fund.
  3. In fact, rate sensitivity can also be read in a negative sense: the fund tends to suffer when rates rise.

This article answers three questions about KBWD:

  1. What kinds of holdings does KBWD have?
  2. How does KBWD fit into an income portfolio?
  3. How do interest rates affect KBWD?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Weak Jobs Data Favors Another Rate Cut In December... Maybe
data.description:
The government shutdown has ended, and new government data will soon start to roll out ahead of the next Federal Reserve policy announcement on Dec. 10. The modest uptick in inflation lately will keep markets guessing, but several estimates of payrolls from private sources in recent days suggest that the case for more easing still has the edge.

Numbers published by ADP and Revelio Labs reflect an ongoing slowdown in hiring in October. A downtrend in nonfarm payrolls is also conspicuous in another estimate through last month by LinkUp.

A rise in job cuts in October is another factor pointing to a bias for a rate cut via recent data. “October’s pace of job cutting was much higher than average for the month. Some industries are correcting after the hiring boom of the pandemic, but this comes as AI adoption, softening consumer and corporate spending, and rising costs drive belt-tightening and hiring freezes. Those laid off now are finding it harder to quickly secure new roles, which could further loosen the labor market,” said Andy Challenger, workplace expert and chief revenue officer for Challenger, Gray & Christmas.

A new forecast from Goldman Sachs estimates that nonfarm payrolls likely fell by 50,000 in October.

But if all this sounds like a slam-dunk for another rate cut, the Fed funds futures market is reflecting new doubts and is now pricing in a coin toss on the central bank’s decision for Dec. 10. That’s a big shift vs. recent history, when the market was clearly leaning in favor of a ¼-point cut in the target rate.

What changed? Lingering inflation concerns are a key factor. Boston Fed President Susan Collins, for example, expressed reluctance for another round of easing in comments on Wednesday:

“Given my baseline outlook, it will likely be appropriate to keep policy rates at the current level for some time to balance the inflation and employment risks in this highly uncertain environment. I see several reasons to have a relatively high bar for additional easing in the near term.”

The outlook, however, is unusually fluid as a cascade of delayed economic reports is scheduled for release now that the government will start publishing reports again. “Markets will face a flood of delayed data releases,” said Jim Reid of Deutsche Bank. “Historical precedent from the 2013 shutdown suggests the September employment report may be among the first to be released, potentially within three business days after reopening.”

David Seif, chief economist for developed markets at Nomura Securities, tells AP that he expects the Fed will keep rates steady in December. “There is a large segment of the Fed that is uncomfortable with a December cut,” he said.

One notable exception: Fed Governor Stephen Miran, who recently said: “If you keep policy this tight for a long period of time, then you run the risk that monetary policy itself is inducing a recession. I don’t see a reason to run that risk if I’m not concerned about inflation on the upside.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UnitedHealth's Rally Still Has Legs, Further Margin Recovery Through 2027
data.description:
I previously covered UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) in August 2025, discussing how its oversold status had been overly done as the market over reacted to the market-wide headwinds arising from the macro uncertainties and the regulatory changes in the healthcare space.

I had believed that UNH was likely to report notable improvements in its performance metrics from 2026 onwards, aided by the raised premiums in the commercial/Medicaid segment, the ongoing operating cost discipline, and the healthy balance sheet, resulting in my reiterated Strong Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss UNH's robust FQ3'25 performance metrics, which suggests a near bottoming in its trough FY2025 year, with the raised insurance premium from 2026 onwards likely to drive a sequential improvement in its margin performance in FY2026.

Despite the outsized recovery from August 2025 levels, I am of the opinion that UNH's rally still has legs, with it resulting in my downgraded rating from Strong Buy to Buy here.

UNH Takes Great Steps To Drive Margin Recovery Through 2027

UNH 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Strong Buy rating, UNH has already rallied by the rich double digits compared to the wider market by the single digits, with a similar recovery also observed in some of its healthcare/insurance peers in varying degrees.

Part of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the factors discussed in my last article, where the management has already hinted at the "pricing strategy is intensely focused on margin recovery and moving back towards our earnings growth targets," with bullish investors also defending a trading floor at the $234s (not too far from the worst of the COVID-19 pandemic bottom of $222s in March 2020) and the subsequent recovery by the double digits since then.

UNH has further fed the rally through the double beat FQ3'25 performance and the raised FY2025 guidance in the recent earnings call.

These naturally validate my last conclusion at which the stock presents a contrarian investment thesis for those with a longer investing trajectory and naturally, a higher risk appetite, attributed to the potentially rich capital appreciation prospect.

For reference, UNH has reported robust FQ3'25 revenues of $113.2B (+1.4% QoQ/+12.3% YoY) and adj EPS of $2.92 (-28.4% QoQ/-59.1% YoY), with the excellent YTD adj EPS outperformance at $14.22 (-31.7%) lending credence to the management's raised FY2025 adj EPS guidance to at least $16.25 (-41.2% YoY).

Much of the tailwinds are attributed to the somewhat stable Medical Care Ratio of 89.9% on a sequential basis (+0.5 points QoQ/+4.7 YoY/+7.4 from FY2019 levels of 82.5%), with it likely presenting a new normal arising from the elevated medical care activity levels, the fee schedule changes, the higher higher morbidity rates, and the aggressive provider coding/billing practices.

Other than the planned raised premiums in the commercial/Medicaid segment from 2026 onwards, UNH has also highlighted other strategies to drive "higher and sustainable double-digit growth beginning in 2027," including:

  • significant insurance benefit adjustments and targeted plan exits for areas without sustainable rates, and
  • narrower physicians/geographies networks.

These reasons are also why the management has expected a relatively drastic reduction in its Medicare Advantage members across the individual and group markets by -1M YoY in 2026, despite being 2 weeks early into the annual enrollment period as of October 28, 2025.

Particularly, in the ACA markets, UNH has submitted the drastically higher prices by over +25% YoY across 30 states to mitigate the higher morbidity rates, not too far from the industry wide raise by +30%, with it implying the management's strategy of delivering a more sustainable/premium member base across its insurance businesses and the consequently improved operating margins nearer to pre-pandemic levels.

Despite the potential top-line impact arising from the member base contraction, the management already expects these efforts to deliver margin improvements from 2026 onwards, up from the current UnitedHealthcare operating margin of 2.1% (-0.3 points QoQ/-3.5 YoY/-3.2 from FY2019 levels of 5.3%) to the "upper half of our 2% to 4% targeted margin range" by 2027.

This is significantly aided by the recently confirmed four stars award for the company's Medicare Advantage plans in the 2027 payment year, allowing them to receive higher annual bonus payments, with the management aiming to continue doing so for the 2028 payment year.

UNH Management Hints At FY2026 Outperformance While Delivering Robust Shareholder Returns

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are also why I continue to expect FY2025 to be a trough year for UNH, with the management likely to offer an excellent FY2026 adj EPS guidance in the upcoming earnings call, as similarly hinted by the management in the earnings call:

While we are still finalizing 2026 plans and intend to share full guidance with you in January, current analyst consensus captures a likely stepping off point for next year. (Seeking Alpha)

For now, the consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $17.70 (+8.5% YoY) already imply an excellent sequential improvement in its bottom-line performance.

Despite the potentially prolonged recovery process, I am of the opinion that UNH remains well capitalized to weather the ongoing industry headwinds, as observed in the rich cash flows from operations at $5.9B in FQ3'25 (-18% QoQ/-57.8% YoY) and YTD at $18.58B (-14.8% YoY).

These remains more than sufficient to cover its annualized dividend obligations of $8B, with the current forward dividend yield of 2.73% still richer compared to the 5Y mean of 1.37% and the sector median of 1.32% - significantly aided by the last payout hike by +5.2%.

I also urge readers to look beyond the QoQ/YoY impact on UNH's balance sheet health to a growing net debt level of $49.51B (-4.9% QoQ/-21.1% YoY), since it is partly attributed to the recently closed Amedisys acquisition worth $3.3B.

This is on top of the management's intensified YTD share repurchases of $5.54B (+37.8% YoY), as they take advantage of the cheap stock prices to return great value to long-term shareholders, with -2.3% of its float already retired over the last twelve months.

UNH's Rally Still Has Legs - Downgrade To Buy

UNH 8Y Stock Price

For now, UNH has had an excellent break out from numerous resistance levels, with it signaling the growing bullish support surrounding its upcoming recovery through 2027, despite the recent inability to break out of the $370s and the subsequent correction arising from the uncertainties surrounding the Affordable Care Act counterproposal.

UNH Valuations

UNH appears reasonable valued at current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 19.88x and my estimated FY2027 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 15.58x as well, based on the current stock prices of $324.21 and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $20.80.

It goes without saying that the stock's rally may still have legs to my bull-case long-term price target of $426.20, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $20.80 and the eventual upgrading to its 5Y P/E mean of 20.49x.

UNH Technical Indicators

A similar trend has been observed in UNH's technical indicators as well, with the moderating RSIs and the relatively low trading volumes implying that the stock is likely to keep much of its recent gains despite the outsized rally from the August 2025 bottom.

As a result of numerous positive factors discussed above, I am of the opinion that its recovery investment thesis remains compelling across dividend incomes and capital appreciation, with it triggering my downgraded rating to a Buy here.

data.title: Munich RE Q3 Earnings Analysis: I Don't Expect It To Outperform
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, I see Münchener Rückversicherungs-Gesellschaft Aktiengesellschaft in München aka Munich Re (OTCPK:MURGY) as a good income play over the long term due to its sustainable dividend and strong fundamentals in the reinsurance industry. However, I grew concerned about one year ago that its earnings momentum could be hurt by catastrophic events in the U.S. Despite that, its operating performance has remained resilient in recent quarters, leading to a positive share price performance since my last article, beating the overall stock market slightly during the same period.

This positive operating backdrop was maintained during the past few quarters, with the company reporting higher earnings and a very good profitability level, showing that its business diversification is key for earnings resilience over the long term.

The company has recently released its third quarter of 2025 (Q3 2025) financial figures, maintaining a very good operating performance across the group. Indeed, its net result in the last quarter amounted to nearly €2 billion, almost doubling from the same quarter of last year. This strong result is mainly explained by lower cat losses, as the company booked losses related to U.S. hurricanes in Q3 2024, especially related to hurricane Helene (about €500 million of losses).

During the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025), its net result was around €5.17 billion, up by 12% YoY, boosted by higher technical result and due to increasing income from investments. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the insurance sector, was 20.8% during 9M 2025, up by 90 basis points (bps) compared to the same period of 2024.

By business segment, Munich RE reported positive momentum across its different business units, with insurance (ERGO) increasing its technical result by 12.5% YoY during 9M 2025 due to lower claims costs, while in the reinsurance segment the company reported much higher earnings driven by much lower cat losses in the P&C segment. Indeed, the combined ratio of the reinsurance P&C segment was quite low in the last quarter (62.7%) and during the first nine months of 2025 (69.8%), as major losses were very low over the past few quarters.

This was the main reason why the technical result increased to more than €4 billion in the reinsurance P&C segment, up 28% YoY. While underwriting results improved markedly over the past few quarters supported by low cat losses, its revenue declined by 6.4% YoY, as pricing has been more challenging in January and July renewals and due to some negative currency effects. This development is not particularly bullish for earnings growth ahead because if major losses normalize it will lead to a higher combined ratio and lower underwriting results. Investors should note that a large part of reinsurance premiums will renew in January and considering that loss events have been subdued in recent months, it’s quite likely in my opinion that pricing will remain negative for the company in the short term.

Regarding its investment result, the company was able to increase it to €5.9 billion in the first nine months of 2025, up by 3% YoY, despite the impact of lower interest rates. As maturing securities are reinvested at higher yields, the investment return of the portfolio continues to increase, even though the reinvestment yield has declined in recent quarters due to lower market rates following the European Central Bank (ECB) cuts to its key rate.

However, as can be seen in the previous graph, Munich RE is still reinvesting at yields above 4%, while its investment return was 4.1% in the last quarter. This means higher investment results may not be easy to achieve in the near future, as reinvestments are made at similar yields to the current portfolio, assuming the asset allocation of its investment portfolio remains more or less unchanged over the coming quarters.

Due to higher technical results and investment returns, Munich RE’s net profit increased by a double-digit rate during 9M 2025, while for the full year the company expects to achieve at least €6 billion, which is unchanged from its previous guidance. This seems to be conservative as Munich RE would only need to achieve a net result of about €800 million in Q4 2025, while current market expectations are for a net result above €1.2 billion in the next quarter. This means its net income in 2025 is likely to be around €6.4 billion, or 7% above its own guidance, representing annual growth of about 13%. Its ROE ratio is expected to be around 19% for the full year, being a good profitability level compared to other reinsurance companies, and an improvement of more than 80 bps compared to 2024.

Regarding its capitalization, Munich RE’s solvency II ratio was 293% at the end of last September, remaining well above its internal target range of between 175-225%. This means the company has an excess capital position and could, theoretically, return all its annual earnings to shareholders. However, reinsurance companies are usually conservative regarding their capital return policies, and Munich RE is no exception.

Its dividend policy is to raise the annual dividend by at least 5% during "normal" years, targeting dividend stability during high-claims years. If catastrophe events remain at usual levels during Q4, this year will be a soft year regarding claims costs, allowing the company to raise its annual dividend by more than its target.

Its last dividend was €20 per share, which is paid only in one instalment like it happens for many European companies, while related to 2025 earnings the current consensus expects Munich RE to increase the annual dividend to €22 per share (+10% YoY). Given that its earnings growth should be around 13% and the company has a very comfortable capital position, I think Munich RE could be more aggressive and increase even further its annual dividend to €23 per share (implying annual growth of 15%) or more, which could be a positive catalyst for its share price ahead.

If Munich RE achieves an annual profit of around 6.4 billion, this implies earnings per share around €49, which means its dividend payout ratio at the expected €22 dividend per share will be 45%. This is a very conservative level, but it’s within the usual payout the company has made in recent years (between 40-50%) and close to the payout ratio in 2024 (46%). However, if the company decides to be more aggressive and return 50% of annual earnings to shareholders, this would lead to an annual dividend of €24.5 per share (+22.5% YoY), which would still be a very sustainable level for a company that has a defensive business profile.

Nevertheless, assuming that Munich RE maintains a more conservative dividend policy and sets its annual dividend at €22 per share, this leads to a forward dividend yield slightly below 4%. This yield is below average compared to the European insurance sector, but it’s similar to its closest peers like Hannover RE (OTCPK:HVRRY) and Swiss RE (OTCPK:SSREY).

Regarding its valuation, Munich RE is currently trading at 2.17x book value, which is close to the top of its historical valuation over the past five years, as can be seen in the next graph.

This means upside potential may be limited ahead, as investment results should be pressured by lower interest rates and 2025 was a very benign year for claims costs, probably leading to slower earnings growth in 2026. Compared to its closest peers, Munich RE is trading at a similar valuation, being another sign that its valuation seems to be fair right now and upside doesn’t seem to be great in the near future.

Conclusion

Munich Re has reported a positive operating momentum in recent quarters, as claim costs have been much lower than usual over the past few months. This was the main reason for higher earnings in recent quarters, a situation that is likely to normalize in the near term, while investment returns should also be at the peak due to lower interest rates in Europe. This makes its investment case largely geared toward income, but Munich RE’s dividend yield is only acceptable compared to its peers. Thus, I maintain my Hold recommendation as I don’t expect Munich RE to outperform the overall stock market ahead, while its sustainable income over the long term is the main reason to hold its shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tencent Q3: This 'China Risk' Stock Should Already Be Trading Higher
data.description:
Introduction

I​‍​‌‍​‍‌ have a previous post from October ("Tencent: China's Sleeping Giant Is Finally Waking Up") about Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY). I described the business as a "sleeping giant" of the Chinese technology sector, which is going to wake up soon. So, the newly released quarterly figures are in line with this statement. Sales increased, net income was double-digit, and the overseas video game business went up by ​‍​‌‍​‍‌40%. Thanks in part to artificial intelligence on WeChat, the advertising business thrived. All of this with little spending, surely lower than the rates of American hyperscalers. Personally, at $84 per ADR, Tencent continues to be priced significantly lower than its peers in its sector, so my rating remains firmly Strong Buy.

The tech giant has woken up.

Earnings season (Q3)

I wasn't expecting a booming quarterly result, and that's exactly what happened. Tencent is entering a phase of normalization that, numbers in hand, is more solid than many would like to admit. As an investor and asset manager, I prefer a company that experiences healthy and consistent growth over one that posts a stellar quarterly report and then declines in subsequent quarters, eroding its stock market gains. However, the Chinese tech giant is growing double-digits across all key lines, maintaining stable margins, and continuing to push AI forward without compromising profitability.

As shown in the "Financial Performance Highlights," quarterly revenues grew 15% to RMB 192.9 billion. So far, so smooth. Digging deeper, I notice that the VAS division posted a solid 16%. If​‍​‌‍​‍‌ anyone is not aware of this historic split, it is about Gaming (the main business of Tencent) and Social Network Services (video and music subscriptions, WeChat, ​‍​‌‍​‍‌etc.), which generally generate the most predictable portion of the company's revenues. Margins are much higher than other business areas like advertising and fintech, so this division is receiving a lot of attention. That's why I immediately glance at this every quarterly Tencent report. If I had to make a comparison, it's the same attention given to AWS as Amazon (AMZN).

Having cleared that, let's get right to the point. Domestic games are increasing by 15%; foreign activities are accelerating by 43%. This rate confirms how Tencent is growing its international footprint yet still keeps its powerful Chinese roots intact.

Margins

On the margin front, Tencent is monetizing AI where it really matters: in profitability. Consolidated gross margin rose to 56%, up from 53% a year ago. In terms of gross margin, the VAS division (Tencent’s core business) stands at 61%, Marketing Services at 57%, and FinTech & Business Services at around 50%, all higher than a year ago. You can see here:

Capex

The first thing that strikes me when looking at these numbers is that while the adjusted EBITDA margin is expanding (from 42% to 45%), Capex is decreasing (from RMB 17,094 to RMB 12,983). You can easily understand why this is an anomaly in this sector, where everyone is spending and competing to see who can invest the most in AI.

In the next graph, you can see the difference in big tech Capex. American hyperscalers (Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta (META)) are seeing their spending increase endlessly, also increasing their debt, which often raises concerns. Tencent, on the other hand, is in a different league. It doesn't need that level of capex to achieve the same operating result. Tencent's AI model is much lighter and less expensive. It integrates monetization directly into existing services (gaming, advertising, payments), so it doesn't have the same infrastructure pressures that Western hyperscalers are experiencing.

In a market that today must reward quality and those who combine AI with profitability as quickly as possible, Tencent is one of the few companies that can demonstrate both simultaneously, and this, in my opinion, is the real strategic differentiator for the next 12–24 months.

Among other things, it may have gone unnoticed, but China is no longer chasing. The gap in AI models has closed much faster than the market expected. And when the outcome is good for both sides, the substantial difference lies in both costs and who manages to monetize first.

Tencent has an advantage here. It doesn't have to spend like the U.S. hyperscalers, because it no longer has a technological gap to catch up with. And it can immediately integrate these models into gaming, advertising, payments, and Mini Shops. AI is no longer a cost, but already a margin.

WeChat

WeChat remains the ecosystem's central asset. I also wrote about this in my October article. In China, if you don't have a smartphone and WeChat, there are many things you can't do. Everything is now digital and concentrated in a single, enormous system that lets you do everything, from paying for metro tickets to booking vacations. All within a single app. I find it incredible.

All this is reflected in the numbers, and in fact, MAUs rose to 1.414 billion, with another 14 million added in the quarter. The company is using WeChat as a platform to push native AI apps and mini-programs that open up new layers of monetization without inflating the cost structure. As an investor, it's clear that capital is now being allocated more selectively.

The deal with Apple (AAPL) fits well in this setting. Apple will handle payments for WeChat apps and mini-games on iOS, charging a 15% commission as opposed to the usual 30%. This means foregoing some of the economics in the very near term, but the trade-off solves a long-running argument, lowers the likelihood of a crackdown on the App Store, and protects a crucial monetization channel. It is a sensible platform trade-off.

Updated valuation

A month ago, I wrote that my fair value was $105 per ADR. As of this writing, the stock is trading at $84.

The interesting thing to note in the next chart is that Tencent is the only global big tech company still stuck on a P/E between 15 and 20, while American big tech companies like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) and Amazon (AMZN) are trading between 28x and 35x.

And the interesting part is that this discount doesn't change even when Tencent has good quarters, increases margins, or generates more cash. It's as if the market continues to price it "out of habit," just because it's a Chinese big tech company. This type of narrative no longer works. Thinking of Tencent as a "Chinese big tech company, low multiple by definition" is now obsolete. The China Discount no longer applies to a company like this. Consider that if Tencent had the same metrics but were listed in the United States, it wouldn't be worth 20x. There's not a problem with the numbers. There's a problem with perception, which will change sooner or later. And the quarterly results provided concrete proof of this.

In this section, I'm updating my target price in light of the new information. As always, I'm starting with a forward P/E of 21.46, which compresses to about 15x by 2028

I'm starting with the Bloomberg consensus 2027 EPS estimate of $4.93 per ADR. I believe this is also an estimate because it incorporates rising AI capex and doesn't assume any margin acceleration from the new gaming pipeline and WeChat Ads. However, I'm using it as a baseline precisely so as not to overstretch the model.

As I said before, I don't believe in China Discount, so the adjusted 2027 P/E ((FWD)) multiple under these conditions is not 17x, but between 22x and 24x. This is the level at which a "global compounder" with stable growth and, above all, low and disciplined capex trades. To be conservative, I choose 22x as the (low-end) multiple to which I apply the estimated EPS of 4.93. At these levels and conditions, I get $108 per ADR. For me, this remains my baseline scenario.

There is a but: the structural contribution of international gaming and AI-based advertising is accelerating earnings at a faster-than-estimated pace. Even without revising the EPS, it is credible to assume that the market will return to pricing Tencent at a minimal premium compared to the median of global tech stocks, so it is not wrong to think that Tencent could be priced at 24x in 2027. At 24x I get a fair value of USD 118 per ADR.

Risks

So far, I've analyzed Tencent's opportunities and positive aspects. We've discussed its low P/E, the importance of AI in its existing business as an accelerator, and its very low capex compared to global competitors. In short, we've analyzed all the positive aspects. Now it's time to analyze the risks. Personally, I see two main ones.

  1. The main and most important factor for me is the China Discount narrative, which could last longer than necessary. The numbers are good, we've seen it, and the quarterly results performed as expected. If global sentiment toward Beijing remains fragile, the multiple may take longer to normalize. This is a risk that investors in Chinese stocks know; it doesn't depend on the balance sheet but on perception. It's a bit annoying, but it will be eliminated over time.
  2. Another risk to consider is cloud and fintech. Margins are there, and growing, but competition from Alibaba (BABA) and ByteDance (BDNCE) is strong, especially on prices. On this path, some problems could arise.

Conclusion

This quarterly report, excluding the noise about China and tariffs, confirms to me that Tencent is an active player in the AI ​​field, with disciplined and careful capital expenditure, not even remotely comparable to American tech companies. Revenues are growing in the mid-teens, margins are expanding, and an ecosystem (WeChat + gaming + fintech) is integrating AI where it truly makes a difference: profitability.

Although its basics are more akin to those of a worldwide quality compounder than an emerging stock, Tencent is considered as a ""discounted Chinese big tech by definition." My reasonable value estimate is now between $108 and $118. The major concern here is the length of time until the market shuts this gap.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seacor Marine: Weak Quarter And Muted Near-Term Prospects - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered SEACOR Marine Holdings Inc., or “Seacor Marine” (SMHI), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

After the close of Wednesday's regular session, leading offshore support vessel provider SEACOR Marine Holdings reported weak third quarter results, with revenues coming in well below consensus estimates.

While the company reported a GAAP profit, this was solely due to the recent sale of two premium liftboats at a substantial gain.

Performance across regions was mixed, with margin improvement in the U.S. market offset by weakness in the North Sea:

In the press release, management attributed the underwhelming performance to a number of issues:

  • downtime resulting from the decision to take the two above-discussed premium liftboats off the market in order to ensure timely delivery to the new owner
  • another premium liftboat still undergoing repairs in the Middle East thus resulting in a negative direct vessel profit (the company's equivalent to gross profit) for this region
  • poor utilization and pricing in the North Sea

On a more positive note, the company's average dayrate of $19,490 was down just slightly from the multi-year high achieved last quarter.

In addition, SEACOR Marine's fast supply vessel ("FSV") fleet saw improved utilization and dayrate performance. The company reactivated two previously cold-stacked U.S. vessels for redeployment in international markets.

More importantly, SEACOR Marine managed to secure multi-year contract awards in Brazil for two of its large hybrid-powered platform supply vessels ("PSVs") with contract commencement scheduled for Q1/2026. The contracts will reduce the company's presence in the difficult North Sea market to just two PSVs.

With Brazil being a high-margin region, average dayrate and direct vessel profit should increase next year.

While operating cash flow for the quarter was negative $10.7 million, the liftboat sale resulted in free cash flow of $56.1 million.

SEACOR Marine intends to use the proceeds for the acquisition of two newbuild PSVs currently under construction in China.

The company ended the quarter with $108.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash as well as $346.4 million in funded debt.

Based on the outlook provided by market leader Tidewater (TDW) last week, 2026 is shaping up to be another challenging year for SEACOR Marine with another drop in offshore drilling activity likely to result in mediocre fleet utilization and persistent margin pressure.

With Tidewater currently preparing for another large-scale acquisition, the company's modern fleet would be an obvious target.

However, based on statements made in the press release, SEACOR Marine appears to be looking for acquisitions itself rather than being acquired:

We are streamlining our cost structure to reflect the recent asset sales and will utilize our improved liquidity profile to fund our newbuild PSV program and position ourselves for developments in offshore markets in the near term.

With a better positioned fleet, an improved cost structure and a strengthened balance sheet, we will continue to explore opportunities to redeploy capital into more attractive assets or consolidation.

At least in my opinion, given the persistent lack of profitability and cash generation, management should consider putting the company on the auction block.

Valuation

With profitability likely to remain challenged for the time being, I am no longer basing my valuation on an EV/EBITDA multiple. Instead, I am using estimated net asset value ("NAV"):

While the recent liftboat sale has resulted in market participants taking notice of SEACOR Marine's deeply discounted valuation, it will likely require the announcement of a formal sales process or dissolution of the company for the NAV discount to decrease significantly.

Under an acquisition scenario, I wouldn't be surprised to see a buyer paying 75% or even 80% of net asset value.

However, it doesn't seem like management would be interested in selling or even dissolving the company at this point.

Given the lack of near-term catalysts, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's shares.

Bottom Line

SEACOR Marine Holdings reported another set of underwhelming quarterly results, with margins and profitability pressured by weak fleet utilization and slightly lower average dayrates.

On the flip side, the report also included some green spots, like improvements in FSV demand and new multi-year contracts for two large PSVs offshore Brazil.

In addition, the recent sale of two premium liftboats resulted in a large one-time gain and boosted liquidity quite meaningfully.

However, with subdued offshore drilling activity likely to persist in the near- to medium-term, the operating environment remains challenging.

While a sale would likely fetch a substantial premium, the company is apparently looking to become an active consolidator rather than being acquired.

SEACOR Marine's weak near-term prospects are well-reflected in the company's deeply discounted valuation, but given the lack of near-term catalysts, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the shares.

data.title: CoreWeave: The Efficiency Paradox Behind Every 'Buy-The-Dip' Rally
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc.’s (CRWV) latest Q3 2025 results have only provided further reinforcement to our previous concerns over the sustainability of its growth model. It’s evident based on the latest results that CoreWeave’s hypergrowth remains out of touch from complementary operational efficiency. This accordingly confirms significant concerns over its scalability – a critical challenge given the inherent capital intensity of its business, which doesn’t mix well with the compressed prime monetization window for its GPU assets due to their rapid advancements.

Although CoreWeave’s delivered 134% y/y revenue growth in the quarter to almost $1.4 billion, progress in its bottom line continues to lag. This accordingly indicates risks of increasing fragility to its underlying growth model, which remains highly dependent on external financing.

Taken together, we believe CoreWeave’s robust headline growth continues to mask structural inefficiencies in underlying operations. The slashed FY 2025 guidance, which management’s attributed to third-party data center build delays, is also a cautionary tale of growing imbalance between CoreWeave’s tightening liquidity and ballooning capex requirements, which shouldn’t be overlooked. Coupled with intensifying insider selling since its post-IPO lockup expiry in mid-August, and recent volatility following the failed Core Scientific (CORZ) acquisition, the stock’s current premium continues to lack durability and remains prone to heightened downside risks.

Heightened Scalability Concerns

CoreWeave’s latest Q3 earnings report has drawn greater doubt over the scalability and sustainability of its current growth mode. While the company has delivered another quarter of stronger than expected revenue growth at 134% y/y to $1.36 billion, the ensuing profit conversion underperformed. Specifically, CoreWeave reported an operating income margin of 4% in Q3, which underperformed the average Wall Street expectation of 6.5%. Although net loss margin improved from -62% in the prior-year quarter and -24% in Q2 to -8% in Q3, the improvements were primarily driven by below-the-line items – notably alleviation in loss on fair value adjustments and expansion in other income compared to prior periods. This is further corroborated by relatively flat adjusted EBITDA and adjusted operating income margins q/q, with compression observed from the prior-year quarter.

The results accordingly highlight the limited margin uplift and scale generated from its persistent headline revenue expansion in recent quarters. While CoreWeave’s been excelling in the build-out of capacity to address immediate demand, it has failed to deliver ensuing efficiency gains, which is critical for unlocking sustainable margin expansion. The company’s effectively monetizing growth at higher marginal costs under its current model, which contradicts the urgency to drive scale and reduce operational dependence on external financing.

This is further exacerbated by the growing misalignment between CoreWeave’s upfront investment outlays required and long-term asset value recoverability prospects. As discussed in one of our previous coverages on the stock, one of the most notable challenges in the neocloud industry is the contrast between their exposure to inherent capital intensity versus the fast-depreciating nature of underlying GPU assets. CoreWeave currently depreciates its underlying GPU assets over a six-year useful life, which contrasts significantly against the prime monetization window of just about two years for each GPU generation.

Although management has attempted to address these concerns directly by noting price resilience on contract renewals for previous-generation GPUs, it’s ironic how this has yet to translate into greater earnings improvement yet.

Demand for AI cloud technology remains robust across generations of GPUs. For example, in Q3, we saw our first 10,000-plus H-100 contract approaching exploration. Two quarters in advance, the customer proactively re-contracted for the infrastructure at a price within 5% of the original agreement.

Source: CoreWeave Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

As the company moves further down the line of each GPU generation’s six-year useful life, the unit economics are only at risk of further deterioration, casting continued doubt over their respective recoverability. This is further corroborated by Amazon Web Services’ (AMZN) recent decision to slash rental prices on its older-generation Nvidia (NVDA) GPU capacity by up to 45%. The decision was made primarily to preserve demand and monetize on available capacity, even though customers may eventually become averse to the older-generation technology’s performance and TCO over the longer-term as R&D advancements persist.

With the Q3 results indicating plateauing margins, even as revenue expands rapidly, it suggests diminishing returns on CoreWeave’s older-generation GPUs while monetization on the newer generation is also poised to follow a similar trajectory. There’s yet to be any evidence that CoreWeave’s been yielding higher profitability with each successive GPU generation, given the lacking earnings improvement to accompany its multi-fold topline growth.

As a result, the latest results continue to highlight fragility in CoreWeave’s underlying growth model. The company continues to face increasing urgency to scale profitably and not just build capacity at all costs to address immediate demand.

Capex Treadmill and Financing Fumbles

CoreWeave’s Q3 results also suggest increasing pressure on its capital structure. The company’s been increasingly focused on structuring its capital profile with interest cost reduction in mind. We believe this strategy is counterintuitive to what it needs to reinforce sustainability of its growth model. By heightening focus on reducing capital costs rather than practicing disciplined capital spend, the strategy highlights an intention to increase reliance on external financing to maintain liquidity and sustain ongoing operations.

Recall that management had initially anticipated full year 2025 capex spend to reach $23 billion, which based on year-to-date outlays imply a $16 billion investment need in Q4 alone. Although the third-party data center build delay has pushed back some of this spending to early 2026, with management now forecasting full year 2025 capex at $14 billion, such levels remain inconsistent with CoreWeave’s available liquidity.

Specifically, the company disclosed total liquidity of about $4.8 billion in its Q2 2025 10Q report. This comprised of about $1.2 billion in cash and cash equivalents and $3.7 billion in undrawn capacity under its existing delayed draw term loan (“DDTL”) and revolving credit facilities. Without incremental borrowing, CoreWeave’s quarterly outflow of about $3.1 billion across operating, investing and financing activities observed in Q3 would’ve exacerbated its liquidity profile.

This spending gap was essentially bridged by additional financing acquired through the $1.75 billion senior notes issued in July; a $400 million upsize to its existing DDTL 2.0 facility which now totals $3 billion; and a newly closed DDTL 3.0 facility that totaled $2.6 billion. The company's also disclosed this week that it's upsized its existing revolving credit facility by $1 billion, with drawable capacity now estimated at about $2 billion - assuming it hasn't added to the $450 million drawn as of June 30.

While these undertakings have preserved CoreWeave’s underlying liquidity, they merely defer rather than solve the company’s ongoing cash flow challenge, as evidenced in the lacking scalability to accompany its breakneck growth. As CoreWeave’s debt profile and revenue continues to expand in lockstep, the increasing leverage exposure essentially renders management’s focus on reducing capital costs a moot point, given the company’s plateauing unit economics and limited operating leverage.

The looming liquidity pressure is amplified by $3.7 billion in debt maturing within the next 12 months, which management attributes primarily to OEM vendor financing and self-amortization loans. However, a deeper dive into 10Q disclosures reveals that the DDTL 1.0 facility, which had an outstanding principal of $1.8 billion at the end of Q2, is already subject to quarterly repayments. Meanwhile, the outstanding principal on its DDTL 2.0 facility, which totalled $5 billion at the end of Q2, is due for repayment under quarterly instalments beginning January 2026. CoreWeave’s also expecting full year 2026 capex to “be well in excess of double that of 2025.” Coupled with ballooning operating spend to support revenue recognition, the company’s exposed to a material increase in fixed cash outflows within the near future, further straining its FCF accretion prospects.

As discussed in our previous coverage on the stock, we had anticipated the failure of CoreWeave’s planned acquisition of Core Scientific to be an adverse development over its liquidity roadmap. The deal would have been a key factor for strengthening CoreWeave’s balance sheet, as it increases its access to additional asset-backed leverage. This is consistent with management’s intention for the business combination to unlock “greater financial flexibility”:

Management’s cited in CoreWeave’s latest earnings update that the failed Core Scientific acquisition will not “adversely impact [its] ability to achieve growth ambitions in the coming years.” However, we believe an indirect headwind pertaining to CoreWeave’s strained financial flexibility persists – an imminent execution risk that far exceeds the lost cost synergy that management had originally anticipated from the transaction.

Taken together, it suggests CoreWeave’s becoming trapped on a capex treadmill, whereby aggressive spending is required to persist in sustaining its current pace of growth. Meanwhile, its ability to translate that business outcome into self-sustaining FCF generation remains in question. CoreWeave is at risk of an imminent financing bottleneck in our opinion. Without Core Scientific’s assets to bolster its balance sheet, the limited scale observed in its existing operations is likely insufficient to secure lenders’ confidence needed to bridge its impending spending gap with low-cost leverage. Meanwhile, tapping into equity financing would introduce share dilution risks and further pressure the stock’s recent selloff. This combination continues to underscore CoreWeave’s limited valuation resilience at current levels, which comes at an inopportune time as investors turn increasingly wary of AI overinvestment.

Aggressive Insider Selling

The string of deepening insider share sales since CoreWeave’s post-IPO lockup expiry in mid-August is also compounding the structural headwinds facing its underlying business and the stock.

Specifically, anchor investor Magnetar Financial has sold almost 28 million shares since August 15, which carry a total value of $3.6 billion. Its stake in CoreWeave has also reduced by about 7% since the company’s IPO, falling from 30.14% to 23.3% based on its latest 13G filing as of September 30. Most of Magnetar’s selling was also concentrated in October, which exceeded 14.1 million CoreWeave shares valued at almost $2 billion. This accordingly highlights risks of another material stake reduction in the current quarter.

The trend risks a worsening risk-off sentiment for the stock amid recent AI bubble concerns, as it potentially retracts confidence in the durability of CoreWeave’s valuation at current levels. The timing just bodes unfavorably against CoreWeave’s earnings underperformance and imminent financing needs, which could risk further amplifying market skepticism over the underlying business’ durability.

Beyond adverse optics, the ongoing string of insider selling also risks tangible implications for CoreWeave’s near-term capital strategy. Ongoing disposals – particularly from anchor investor Magnetar, which wasn’t part of any previously disclosed 10b5-1 insider selling plan – could increase supply of CoreWeave shares in the market and limit its upside potential. This could inadvertently weigh on CoreWeave’s ability in commanding attractive pricing on any share issuances in the near-term needed to fund ongoing operations and growth investments and heighten the extent of impending dilution risks.

Final Thoughts

CoreWeave’s latest Q3 results have illustrated the increasing divergence between its ability in capturing robust demand and maintaining long-term operational sustainability. The company’s persistent streak of multi-fold growth remains an impressive feat, highlighting consistent execution in capturing robust hyperscaler AI infrastructure spending – an opportunity that’s expected to approach $400 billion this year with further increases anticipated in the next. Yet the failure to convert said growth into meaningful margin expansion underscores a structural imbalance in its business model that hinders long-term FCF accretion. We believe CoreWeave’s liquidity trajectory remains precarious, as its recent operating leverage improvements remain insufficient in driving meaningful scale, while elevated capex spend adds pressure to its near-term financing needs.

As discussed earlier, the cancelled Core Scientific transaction, alongside recent price pressure on the stock have also compressed CoreWeave’s financing flexibility. This is further compounded by steepening insider share sales in recent months, which risks tempering investors’ confidence further.

Admittedly, there are still counteractive drivers to our downside risk worth considering. Access to non-dilutive funding at low capital costs; stake expansion by existing anchor investors – such as Nvidia; and/or faster-than-expected revenue recognition after resolving the third-party data center build delay remain potential upside catalysts in the near-term. The stock’s valuation multiple premium has also corrected significantly since before its lockup expiry, and more evidently in recent weeks.

However, the key valuation multiple compression overhang – namely, its lacking scalability and visibility into long-term operational sustainability – would remain unaddressed under the above-stated upside scenarios. Until CoreWeave proves its growth model can generate sustained, scalable returns without ballooning reliance on external capital, the stock’s risk-reward profile remains skewed to the downside at current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Baytex Energy: Strategy Change For Better Valuation
data.description:
Baytex Energy (BTE) announced the sale of the Eagle Ford assets in an attempt to refocus the company as a Canadian company with some high growth possibilities. Management had long expressed dissatisfaction with the action of the stock price since the acquisition of the Eagle Ford properties. Therefore, a change in strategic direction was indicated. The sale price is $2.3 billion (or C$3.25 billion). This will enable a paydown of the bank line and the 2030 bonds. Supposedly, it will improve some key ratios as well to result in a strengthened financial position.

Last Article

This sale represents a significant strategy change that was followed as late as the last earnings report covered in the last article.

This strategy change likely came about due to the action of the stock price combined with the "too slow" growth of free cash flow.

With the bargaining between Canada and the United States marked by threats of tariffs and trade wars, there was some insulation of earnings due to the considerable presence in the United States of Eagle ford light oil production. But that advantage is now being sold.

Stock Price

Evidently the market is receptive to the idea as shown below:

There will still be one set of bonds outstanding. But so far as shown above, the market is more than ok with a simple structure that results from the sale. It may be actually a lot easier for the market to understand.

Corporate Breakeven Decline
